entry.title: Mapping demo
entry.content:
After many rounds of iteration, we have successfully implemented an autonomous exploration and mapping demo, demonstrating the capability of our platform.

  • LiDAR point clouds are mapped into a gridmap (2 cm/grid)
  • Wheel encoder and IMU sensor fusion for accurate odometry
  • Exploration algorithm to prioritize unexplored frontiers
  • Path planning from current pose to goal pose
  • Path following with PID motor control
  • Multi-threaded, all compute is done on phone
  • Remote visualization and emergency stop/teleop over websocket
entry.summary: After many rounds of iteration, we have successfully implemented an autonomous exploration and mapping demo, demonstrating the capability of our platform.

entry.title: Building the Inner Ring of the Demo Environment

link.title: Building the Inner Ring of the Demo Environment

entry.content:
We are making progress on building out our demo environment. Nathan was able to design corner, T, and connection brackets for the wood paneling, and Anders was able to buy and cut the plywood we needed. Olivia 3D printed the brackets out, and we now have an environment that Kai and Thomason can start testing our algorithms in.

The beginning of our demo environment built out.
entry.summary: Building the Inner Ring of the Demo Environment

entry.title: New Mechanical Design
entry.content:
This week, we made lots of progress on our updated mechanical design. Anders designed roamr V2, an upgrade from roamr V1 originally designed by Nathan.

Here, we decided to maintain the burger design of V1, but added more space between the two panels to slot the battery inside as well as the electronics. Furthermore, we added a optical breadboard-like mounting hole pattern on the two panels, making it easy to shift the mounting of different components around and add new parts.

New mechanical design.

Additionally, we figured out the mounting and shape that the PCBs should be.

New mechanical design with PCB mounting.

entry.title: New Electrical Design
entry.content:
Today, Thomason and Olivia came together to brainstorm the new electrical architecture, shifting away from OTS components to a custom designed PCB based on a modular design.

We decided to develop two PCBS - a power PCB that interfaces between the rest of the hardware and the battery, and the motor PCB, which will interface between the iPhone commands and the motors. With the exception of the power PCB, each subsequent PCB will have its own microcontroller, enabling a node-based approach to our goal of hardware extensibility.

entry.title: Demo Ideation
entry.content:
Today, we came together to decide what we wanted to show for the demo. We wanted to showcase the capabilities of our software.

We decided on a two-part demo. The inner square, which will be 4x4 ft, will feature the robot learning the environment as we rearrange the obstacles in various configurations. The outer ring, the robot will run through a series of obstacles, demonstrating perception and autonomous decision-making capabilities.

Inner and outer environment for the robot demo.

entry.title: Communication and Interfacing
entry.content:
• Added Bluetooth connection response to the ESP-32, which will allow teleoperated control from the phone, and is also required for WASM communicating and running with motors.
  • Added communication from phone to any browser, allowing a user to teleoperate when the phone is attached to a robot.

entry.title: Integration
entry.content:
The whole team got together to put together a working prototype of the robot.

The team hard at work.








roamr v0!

entry.title: Software Infrastructure
entry.content:
We switched from the third-party WASMKit library to the officially supported WebAssembly WAMR library due to the lack of support in WASMKit for multi-threading. Now, WAMR allows us to compile into a file that can be interfaced with our app. Inside the WAMR library, we enabled certain flags that will become useful for our robot development:

  • Shared memory
  • Threads
  • Neural networks

Thomason also built out a feature that lets us call Swift function bindings from C++ code, thereby minimizing the amount of Swift code that users will have to touch in the future.
entry.summary: WAMR backend by Thomason and Anders

entry.title: BLE Demo
entry.content:
Olivia created a basic demo to show basic Bluetooth Low Energy phone-to-microcontroller communication.

  • ESP hooked up to laptop for serial monitoring
  • Connecting to the ESP32 through my phone using NRC-connect and sending it a message
  • The ESP-32 gets the message over BLE and laptop prints it out on the terminal serial monitor

entry.title: Basic roamr app
entry.content:
To validate our sensor-reading ability through iOS APIs, Anders created a basic app (roamr v0.0!) that reads and displays the following:

  • LiDAR data: mesh visualization and new point count
  • Accelerometer: x, y, z acceleration
  • Gyro visualization
First iteration of roamr app
